sections.assessment: This chapter has a style — spare, austere, forensically precise — but it barely has a voice in the King sense. King's narrators are human presences. They editorialize. They take sides. They occasionally break the surface with dry humor or moral alarm. You know someone is home. Here, the narrator is almost entirely absent as a personality, functioning instead as a cold recording device of exceptional accuracy. The closest analogue is Hilary Mantel's Wolf Hall — stripped syntax, present-weight detail, withheld judgment — and that comparison is a compliment on craft but a problem on King's specific standard. The prose is never sloppy or 'literary' in the bad sense, and the restraint does create genuine dread: the reader has to supply the horror of what Augustine is doing because the narrator refuses to name it. But King would say: you've worked so hard to disappear that I've lost the feeling of someone telling me a story. The narrator flickers into personality exactly three times, and each time it's startling and devastating precisely because it's so rare: 'The reference was apt. The reference was a little merciless.' / 'he had subsequently been progressively pretending the line did not bear.' / 'He slept well.' Those three moments are the voice this whole chapter should be running on. The dossier-style character introductions for Peter and Mark, the neutral clinical framing of Augustine's political maneuvering, the Cirta funeral rendered without a single editorial flicker — these sections work as plotting but the narrator has gone silent as a person. You can be austere and still have a voice. Cormac McCarthy is austere. His narrators still have a moral weather. This one mostly doesn't.
good_examples: 'Augustine sat with the line. The line was his own line. The line had been his own line for sixteen years... he had subsequently been progressively pretending the line did not bear.' — The repetition of 'the line' and the buried knife of 'progressively pretending' is the narrator speaking. Brief, cold, merciless. This is the voice.

'He slept well.' — Two words that do the work of a paragraph. The narrator trusts the reader completely and delivers the chapter's moral verdict in the most understated form possible. King would applaud this.

'The reference was apt. The reference was a little merciless.' — The only place where the narrator volunteers an opinion on what it's watching. The doubled sentence construction performs the very precision it's describing. More of this.
bad_examples: 'Mark was twenty-four, the son of a small ironmonger from Calama, ordained to the diaconate in the spring of the previous year on Possidius's recommendation, and presently assigned to the Hippo basilica for advanced training in liturgy and correspondence.' — This is biography, not narration. The voice has gone bureaucratic. King would compress and inject one telling detail with heat instead of a dossier.

'He came up by foot from the harbour with one companion, on the morning of the feast of the African martyrs, having sent no advance message.' — The opening sentence is functional and cold. It delivers information. It does not signal a personality behind the telling. King opens with presence: someone leaning forward to say 'listen.'

The entire Cirta funeral sequence — Augustine weeps, carries the bier, means the homily, returns home and immediately resumes the destruction of Pelagius — and the narrator has no reaction to the coexistence of these things. The moral vertigo available here is the whole point of the chapter, and the narrator lets it pass without a flicker of acknowledgment. King would find one sentence — one — to press the reader's face into it.
suggestions: Give the narrator one additional moment of dry, moral editorial per scene-unit — not judgment exactly, but the verbal equivalent of a raised eyebrow. The Peter scene and the Mark scene both end with Augustine framing his actions as necessity; the narrator accepts the framing silently. A single sentence of deflation — the way 'He slept well' deflates the entire final section — would not break the chapter's austerity but would establish that someone is home.

The character introductions for Peter and Mark read like ecclesiastical records. Cut the biographical scaffolding to one or two details and load those details with the narrator's angle of vision. 'He was twenty-two and had not yet learned to read his bishop's pauses for the matter behind the procedure' — that line already exists in the chapter and it's the best character sentence in both introductions. The dossier sentences before it are throat-clearing.

The narrator currently has no consistent tonal signature the way great King narrators do — the slight dark humor, the moment of complicity, the sense that the narrator has seen this kind of thing before and knows what it means. Consider whether the narrator should carry a very slight note of the historian who already knows how this ends — not omniscient commentary, but the occasional word choice that implies the speaker has been living with these events for a long time and has made a judgment about them.

sections.assessment: This chapter operates like a camera with a variable zoom: it pulls back to chronicle distance for long stretches, then plunges without warning into Augustine's skull with terrifying intimacy. That architecture is partly intentional and partly a missed opportunity. When the chapter goes deep, it goes very deep — the 'wrapper' monologue, the Peter observation, the final 'he slept well' — these are among the finest POV moments in historical fiction, full stop. Augustine's mental voice is specific, lawyerly, self-aware to the point of self-disgust, and King's principle that the reader must be behind the character's eyes is honored completely in those passages. But the chapter earns those depths infrequently. The long theology debate is witnessed from outside; we watch two brilliant men argue rather than inhabiting Augustine's nervous system as Pelagius dismantles him argument by argument. We are told he 'thought of his mother' five times in five words each, which is reported thought masquerading as rendered thought. Filtering verbs — 'registered,' 'watched,' 'composed' — keep us one pane of glass away from full immersion. The chapter's best instinct is to trust the reader with unmediated consciousness; its recurring weakness is retreating to the safe distance of the observer-narrator when the theology gets thick.
good_examples: 'He thought one thing only, and he thought it in the steady internal voice of the work: *If I let him go to Palestine and let his writings circulate with the obvious truth of his face — within ten years there will be a Pelagian Latin Church that will eat my work. The work cannot be eaten. The work is the wrapper. The wrapper has to hold. I will have to destroy him.*' — The phrase 'steady internal voice of the work' is character-specific and chilling. 'The work is the wrapper' is how THIS man thinks — aphoristic, self-convincing, compressed. No filtering verb. Pure unmediated consciousness. This is King's ideal: thought experienced, not reported.

'He has decided. By the second week he will be marking passages without the small pause. By the third month he will be a young man of a particular kind, and the kind will not unlearn what it has learned tonight in the next forty years of his life. This is what I do to the young men.' — Free indirect discourse at its most devastating. 'This is what I do to the young men' is the voice of a man who has watched himself do this before. No 'he realized.' No 'he thought with shame.' The self-knowledge is rendered, not summarized.

'The line was his own line. The line had been his own line for sixteen years... Pelagius was now handing it back to him with a reading that the line plainly bore, that he himself had written the line to bear, and that he had subsequently been progressively pretending the line did not bear.' — The repetition of 'the line' mimics obsessive cognition. 'Progressively pretending' is Augustine's specific register of philosophical self-examination turned against himself. This is a mind we are inside, not watching.

'Inside the part of himself that was not a bishop he flinched.' — Eleven words. King would call this the sentence that earns everything around it. The layered self — bishop-self and private-self — is rendered in real time, not explained.

'He slept well.' — The chapter's closing kicker. Two words that contain everything the preceding 7,000 words have built. This is King's 'invisible prose' principle at full power: the sentence does nothing except destroy the reader.
bad_examples: 'Augustine, watching him, registered the not-eating without comment. The cleanliness of the wool of the tunic...' — 'Registered' is a filtering verb that holds us outside Augustine's perception. Cut 'watching him, registered.' Start directly with the observations: 'The wool was clean — washed in cold water, no fuller. The small darn at the hem...' We're inside his noticing, not watching him notice.

'He thought of his mother. He thought of Ambrose. He thought of his teacher Simplicianus. He thought of Possidius. He thought of Stephen. He thought, against his choosing, of the man in front of him.' — This is reported thought five times over. We're told he had thoughts; we experience none of them. What does his mother's face look like right now in this courtyard? What does Ambrose's voice sound like in his memory? King would break through at least one of these into actual content. The accumulation tells us the thoughts existed; it does not give us the thoughts.

'He composed an answer. He gave it in the language of the technical position he had been working out for ten years.' — 'Composed' is external. We watch Augustine from outside his skull as he formulates. The half-hour speech that follows is entirely witnessed, not inhabited. Where is his body during this speech? What is he feeling while the words come out? Is any part of him surprised by his own argument? The POV exits at the exact moment it should deepen.

'The morning was bright, with a thin white sky of the kind that comes at Hippo in October...' — Beautiful, historically precise, and written from no one's consciousness. Compare this to what it could be: the specific way Augustine registers the October light given what he is about to do, given who is standing in it. The description earns its place aesthetically but earns no POV points.
suggestions: During Pelagius's arguments, give us Augustine's gut. Not after — during. When Pelagius says 'The heart rests because the heart was made for the resting,' Augustine must be feeling something — recognition, danger, the specific dread of a man whose best sentence is being turned against him. One or two italicized interior beats embedded in the dialogue would keep us inside his skull through the long theological exchange.

Replace the 'He thought of his mother / He thought of Ambrose' sequence with one fully rendered memory — twenty words of actual recalled image or voice — and let that one memory carry the weight of all six. 'He thought of his mother, and of the afternoon at Ostia, and of her face when she said she had nothing left to want' is inside the memory. 'He thought of his mother' is outside it.

Audit every 'registered,' 'watched,' 'noticed,' 'observed,' and 'caught himself.' These are the glass between reader and character. In most cases the filtering verb can be deleted and the observation given directly: not 'he registered the not-eating' but 'the figs sat untouched.' Trust the reader to understand the bishop sees what is in front of him.

The chapter's opening two paragraphs are written in the register of a chronicle — no interiority, no consciousness. Consider entering Augustine's POV at the moment the message arrives: the pen set down, the specific quality of that moment's stillness. The reader needs to be anchored in his body before the intellectual drama begins.

The line 'The meaning had not at any point been incompatible with the work that was waiting on the upper shelf' is brilliant self-justification rendered in free indirect discourse — but it arrives only at the chapter's end. Seed the earlier rationalization machinery in smaller beats throughout. Let us watch the justification-building begin earlier, so that final line lands as culmination rather than revelation.

sections.assessment: This chapter is doing something genuinely difficult and mostly doing it right: characterizing historical figures under intellectual and moral pressure without ever letting the history lesson swallow the people. The best characterization here works exactly the way King insists it should — through action, through the gap between public and private selves, through what characters choose to do in the moment rather than what they tell us about themselves. Augustine checking the hidden disc in Cassian's Conferences before going to the courtyard is worth ten paragraphs of interior monologue about his caution and guilt. Augustine drinking what Pelagius left in the cup is worth a thesis on his ambivalence. 'He slept well' — three words to close a chapter in which a man has arranged a death — is as ruthless and perfectly placed as any King ending. The Possidius exchange ('Cirta first?' / 'No. Rome first.') reveals a decades-long partnership through compression so tight it approaches shorthand. The chapter's central achievement is showing us a man who genuinely means his homily, genuinely weeps for his dead friend, and is simultaneously routing a good man to a desert prison — and doing this through action and juxtaposition, never through Augustine explaining his own contradictions. The failure point is the introduction of secondary characters: Peter and Mark both get resume-style biographical entries that tell us their fathers' professions and their ecclesiastical ranks before they've done a single thing to earn our attention. These are exactly the kind of backstory dumps King warns against. Peter's pausing stylus on the wax is more characterization than his parentage and the scriptorium hierarchy. The stutter detail for Mark is good King material — specific, physical, involuntary — but then gets over-explained rather than trusted to work.
good_examples: 'When Eraclius had gone, Augustine sat at the desk for a count of perhaps twenty. He went to the lower shelf, lifted Cassian's Conferences from between the desert fathers, parted the binding by an eighth of an inch, and looked.' — Pure action backstory: the disc, the packet, the letter, his careful replacing of the threads. We understand everything about this man's situation and state of mind without a word of explanation.

'He thought one thing only, and he thought it in the steady internal voice of the work: If I let him go to Palestine and let his writings circulate with the obvious truth of his face — within ten years there will be a Pelagian Latin Church that will eat my work. The work cannot be eaten. The work is the wrapper. The wrapper has to hold. I will have to destroy him. Then he said, aloud: Pelagius. The light is going.' — The gap between the interior thought and the spoken sentence does everything. No narration needed. The character is the gap.

'The reference was apt. The reference was a little merciless. Augustine took the small mercilessness without flinching outwardly. Inside the part of himself that was not a bishop he flinched.' — Character revealed under pressure in three sentences, with the telling subdivision: 'the part of himself that was not a bishop.'

'By the second week he will be marking passages without the small pause. By the third month he will be a young man of a particular kind, and the kind will not unlearn what it has learned tonight in the next forty years of his life. This is what I do to the young men.' — This is backstory as moral indictment delivered through future projection. We understand Augustine's full history with the young men he shapes without being told any of it.

'He wept, briefly, when the bier went into the crypt. He did not weep as a public gesture.' — Four words, 'not a public gesture,' carry the weight of everything the chapter has established about Augustine's public and private selves.

Augustine drinking from Pelagius's cup after the courtyard empties — no explanation offered, none needed. The action is complete. It is almost sacramental. It is also a little like a man eating evidence.

'He slept well.' — The chapter's final three words. After arranging a man's death. After meaning the homily. After cutting the qualifications from Pelagius's text. Three words that King would recognize as the correct place to put the period and walk away.
bad_examples: 'Peter was twenty-two, the second son of a small landowner from the south, a sub-deacon of the basilica, and the cleanest hand in the scriptorium after the boy who was making the fair copy of the Confessions for Paulinus.' — Resume delivery. 'Second son of a small landowner' tells us nothing about who Peter is. We learn Peter from his stylus pausing on the wax. Lead with that.

'Mark was twenty-four, the son of a small ironmonger from Calama, ordained to the diaconate in the spring of the previous year on Possidius's recommendation, and presently assigned to the Hippo basilica for advanced training in liturgy and correspondence. He was a slight, dark, eager young man with a quick smile and a slight stutter on initial p's that he had been working since boyhood to control and that emerged only when he was nervous. He was nervous now. He had been called to the writing room without notice and he believed, on the evidence of the speed of the summons, that he was about to be given a delicate and important commission, which he had been hoping for since spring. He was correct that he was about to be given one.' — Three separate problems collapsed into one entry: the ironmonger father is irrelevant, the stutter explanation over-explains what the stutter should show, and 'He was correct that he was about to be given one' is the author stepping in to confirm what the reader has already understood. Cut the last sentence entirely. Let the stutter's absence at the end ('The stutter had not appeared') do its work without the setup paragraph.

'Lawrence, the bishop of Cirta, who had been a friend of Augustine's since the years at Carthage when both were students under the same grammarian and who had stood as one of the consecrating bishops at Augustine's episcopal ordination in 395, died at Cirta in the second week of December...' — This is the annual report version of a death notice. The grammarian and the ordination date are not what makes Lawrence's death matter. The homily Augustine subsequently delivers, where he talks about the letter Lawrence wrote him in the year of his consecration, delivers the same backstory through love and grief. Let the homily introduce Lawrence. Drop the subordinate clause.
suggestions: Cut Peter's introduction to two sentences of functional fact and open his scene with the action that defines him: the stylus pausing on the wax. Everything else — who his father was, his ecclesiastical rank — can be absorbed into the action or dropped. We learn his status through the way Augustine speaks to him.

The stutter detail for Mark is earned and specific — keep it, but trust it. Cut 'He was nervous now. He had been called to the writing room without notice and he believed, on the evidence of the speed of the summons, that he was about to be given a delicate and important commission, which he had been hoping for since spring. He was correct that he was about to be given one.' All of this is over-explanation of what the stutter already shows and what the scene will immediately demonstrate. Start with Mark at the door, stutter implied, hope implied through his bearing.

The Lawrence death notice would hit harder if the biographical subordinate clause were moved to the homily, which is where Augustine earns the right to speak that history. 'Lawrence, the bishop of Cirta, died at Cirta in the second week of December of a quartan fever' — period. Let the grief and the history unfold in Augustine's own words at the bier.

The Pelagius theological exchange is doing double duty — characterizing both men's minds while advancing the thriller plot — and it earns its length. But consider whether the section where Augustine gives 'the position he had been working out for ten years' needs its full half-hour in summary. We already know Augustine's position from the earlier exchange. What we need is Pelagius's final move and Augustine's silence. The summarized rebuttal slightly dilutes the payoff of 'You read the line attentively.'

The Mark dispatch scene, in which Augustine reflects on what the sealed letters actually contain, is among the chapter's most efficient characterization — the slow revelation of the death-sentence wrapped in doctrine is excellent. But the line 'The two facts were not in tension, in the sense that the bishop required of facts that they not be in tension' is the kind of authorial interpretive gloss King would cut. The facts speak for themselves. Let the reader feel the irony without being handed it.

sections.assessment: This chapter contains some genuinely excellent sensory work — specific, located, doing double duty — surrounded by large passages where the physical world goes almost entirely dark. The courtyard scene is the crown jewel: the October light, the fig tree bearing late, the calloused feet, the ambient sound of prayers drifting from the cloister. These are King-level details. They make the meeting feel real and they carry moral weight simultaneously. But the writing room — the chapter's most important location, where all the corruption is planned and enacted — has no smell, no temperature, no sound. It is pure furniture. The funeral cathedral at Cirta is described in visual inventory only: clergy in choir, body on bier, candles. We never feel that packed December space. The road to Cirta passes without weather, dust, or cold. The sea is referenced throughout the chapter as a destination and a departure point but is never rendered — not one gull, not one smell of salt. King's principle is that setting is a character that pushes back against your people. Here the physical world exists only to validate the intellectual action, and it disappears entirely whenever the prose moves indoors or into motion. The result is a chapter that is brilliant in patches and disembodied in bulk.
good_examples: "The morning was bright, with a thin white sky of the kind that comes at Hippo in October when the autumn wind has just begun to pull off the sea, and the courtyard held the light without holding the heat." — Triple duty: season, atmosphere, and a physical metaphor for the meeting itself — warm-seeming, ultimately cold.

"From somewhere inside the cloister a voice was reading aloud — Stephen, probably, at the prayers — and the cadence of the reading came faintly across the courtyard and went away." — The only ambient sound in the chapter, and it lands perfectly: the liturgy bleeds in while Augustine is deciding to destroy a man.
"The soles on the courtyard stones were as calloused as the feet of a working ploughman." — King's telling detail in its purest form. One physical fact that does the work of a paragraph of biography.
"The water tasted of nothing in particular." — Understated and precise. The sensory absence is the point. The moral weight lands because the writer trusts the reader and doesn't explain it.

"a small darn at the hem in a slightly different colour" — The kind of specific observation that makes Augustine's surveillance of Pelagius feel real and slightly sinister. You see him cataloguing the man.
bad_examples: "When Eraclius had gone, Augustine sat at the desk for a count of perhaps twenty. He went to the lower shelf, lifted Cassian's *Conferences* from between the desert fathers, parted the binding by an eighth of an inch, and looked." — The writing room is the moral center of the chapter and it has no sensory life. No smell of parchment or tallow, no stone-cold floor, no quality of light. Pure action, no place.

"The cathedral at Cirta was small and packed. Lawrence's clergy were in the choir. Those preparing for baptism stood at the sides. The lay congregation filled the nave to the back." — Visual inventory only. A packed December cathedral in North Africa should press on the reader: incense, candle heat, body warmth against cold stone, the smell of wool. It's a funeral. It should cost us something physically.

"They rode the first stage in silence." — The journey to Cirta in December is a single clause. No cold, no road dust, no sound of horses on winter ground. King would have given us two sentences and we'd have felt the season.

"He carried the bier with the others, his hand under the wood at the right shoulder" — Good physical action but the moment asks for one more sensory anchor: the weight of the wood, the cold of the crypt, something that makes the grief in Augustine's body real rather than reported.
suggestions: Give the writing room a sensory identity and keep it consistent every time we enter it: the smell of parchment and iron ink and lamp oil, the cold of stone in the small hours, maybe a particular sound from the basilica at night. This room is where Augustine becomes what he becomes — it should feel like a place with a moral atmosphere.

The December funeral at Cirta needs at least one olfactory detail — incense, cold stone, the particular smell of packed bodies in wool. You already have the visual inventory. Add one thing we can smell or feel and the scene earns its emotional weight.

The sea is doing narrative work throughout this chapter (ships, tides, departures, destinations) but it never appears as a physical presence. When Pelagius and his companion walk 'down toward the harbour in the slanting light,' that's the moment — one line of salt air, one sound, would anchor everything the harbour represents.

Let December feel like December. Once the chapter moves from the October courtyard meeting into the weeks of plotting and the December funeral, the season needs to register in the body — cold floors, cold ink, cold roads. The moral temperature dropping alongside the actual temperature is there for the taking.

The lamp-lit night scenes with Peter being corrupted are the most morally loaded passages in the chapter. Lamplight flickers. It throws shadows. Burning tallow has a smell. These physical facts, deployed in those scenes, would do the work that no amount of interiority can do as efficiently — they'd make the corruption feel like something happening in a real place to a real boy.

sections.assessment: This chapter builds dread the way good ice forms — invisibly, from the bottom up, until the surface looks perfectly still and everything underneath has already changed. The author understands what King has always understood: horror is not the monster arriving, it is the knowledge that the monster has already decided. The chapter's engine is dramatic irony delivered in a prose so calm it functions as a weapon. We watch Pelagius walk into a room where Augustine has already, by the end, sentenced him to die in an Egyptian desert — and Pelagius never knows. The pivot scene ('I will have to destroy him') drops approximately two-thirds through the courtyard sequence, and it works because the author has spent six thousand words making us like Pelagius, making us feel the intellectual justice of his argument, making us watch Augustine almost yield. The dread that follows is retrospective: we re-read everything Pelagius said knowing it cost him his life. The secondary tension engine is the corruption of Peter — the chapter gives us Augustine's internal autopsy of what he's doing to the boy in real time: 'By the third month he will be a young man of a particular kind, and the kind will not unlearn what it has learned tonight.' That sentence is cold horror. The institutional machinery of evil — excerpts stripped of their qualifications, sealed letters, a deacon who delivers in good faith — is more disturbing than any violence because it mirrors how the world actually works. The chapter closes on 'He slept well,' two words that do the work of a chapter. The flaw is the companion, a figure introduced with considerable eeriness (never sits, never eats, never speaks, bows once and goes to the wall) and then essentially abandoned. That figure should become unbearable by the end — he witnessed everything and will carry it with him, or he is Pelagius's own Peter, or he becomes the man on the road Possidius reports. The story doesn't use him and misses a second instrument of dread.
good_examples: 'He went to the lower shelf, lifted Cassian's Conferences from between the desert fathers, parted the binding by an eighth of an inch, and looked. The disc was where he had laid it. The leather packet was where he had laid it. The folded letter from Anthony was where he had laid it.' — King's 'ordinary made unsettling' in its purest form. We don't know what these objects are. Augustine checking them before meeting a theologian tells us this is not a theological meeting. The prose stays flat; the dread is entirely in the reader's imagination.

'If I let him go to Palestine and let his writings circulate with the obvious truth of his face — within ten years there will be a Pelagian Latin Church that will eat my work. The work cannot be eaten. The work is the wrapper. The wrapper has to hold. I will have to destroy him.' — The reveal is clinical, bureaucratic, delivered in the same register as Augustine's theological arguments. No rage, no anguish, just calculation. That's the horror. King would note this: evil that sounds like a to-do list is more frightening than evil that rages.

'Will we meet again. / I think not. / Pelagius nodded slowly. I think not also. He did not say it sadly. He said it in the tone of a man recording a probable fact.' — Devastating dramatic irony. Pelagius reads 'I think not' as an acknowledgment of geography. The reader reads it as a death sentence. The prose's refusal to flinch ('recording a probable fact') is what makes it land.

'By the second week he will be marking passages without the small pause. By the third month he will be a young man of a particular kind, and the kind will not unlearn what it has learned tonight in the next forty years of his life. This is what I do to the young men.' — Augustine narrating his own corruption of the innocent is a horror-craft move. King does this with Flagg: the monster who knows what he is and continues anyway. 'This is what I do to the young men' is the most chilling line in the chapter.

'He slept well.' — Two words at chapter's end carrying the weight of everything above. Augustine has ordered a man's death, corrupted a boy, and dispatched sealed letters containing murder instructions. He slept well. This is King's principle that the truly frightening thing is normalcy on the far side of evil.
bad_examples: 'The reference was apt. The reference was a little merciless.' — The author editorializing on what we just watched. We don't need to be told the reference was a little merciless; we felt it. King's invisible prose rule: if you've done the work, don't announce that you've done it. Cut both sentences; end on 'Inside the part of himself that was not a bishop he flinched.'

'He had not yet learned to read his bishop's pauses for the matter behind the procedure.' — Telling us Peter is naive rather than letting us watch it. The boy's hesitation, his half-question, his eventual compliance — those already show us his naivety. This sentence double-parks. One showing, no telling.

The companion's three-hour vigil — introduced with eerie precision ('He did not, in three hours, sit down or accept food') but then completely abandoned after Pelagius leaves. This is a missed instrument of dread. A young man who watched everything, whose face Stephen later spots near the basilica gate, should carry escalating unease. Instead he vanishes. The surveillance detail ('He saw the man at second prayers from the bell tower') is a return that works — but it should pay off the companion's earlier strangeness more explicitly.

'He was nervous now. He had been called to the writing room without notice and he believed, on the evidence of the speed of the summons, that he was about to be given a delicate and important commission, which he had been hoping for since spring.' — The Mark sequence rushes its character setup. Two sentences of flatly reported interiority ('he believed... he had been hoping for') replace what should be shown: the stutter appearing and disappearing, the smile he can't suppress. The dread in the Mark scene — that he is unknowingly delivering a death warrant — requires us to feel how clean and eager and unaware he is. We're told he's eager; we need to watch it.
suggestions: Use the companion as a sustained dread instrument. Give him one action at the moment of Pelagius's departure — perhaps he picks up the wooden cup Pelagius set down, looks at it, sets it back — something that shows he has registered what Pelagius has not. Then let Stephen's bell-tower sighting be clearly this same young man, now watching, so the reader understands Pelagius has sent someone back. This doubles the stakes: Augustine is moving against Pelagius, and Pelagius may know it.

The disc and the leather packet and the folded letter from Anthony inside Cassian's Conferences must pay off, either in this chapter or the next. As written they function as a promise the chapter doesn't keep. King's rule: the gun must fire. If these are the thriller's MacGuffin, give us one more beat where Augustine's hand moves toward the shelf after the Peter dictation — and stops. Let him choose not to check. That motion, interrupted, tells us everything.

Strip the editorial commentary. Cut 'The reference was apt. The reference was a little merciless.' Cut 'He had not yet learned to read his bishop's pauses.' Trust the scenes you've built. When the prose editorializes it breaks the dream — the reader is suddenly watching a narrator rather than living the moment.

The transition from courtyard to Peter needs one beat of Augustine alone with his decision. As written, we go from 'He sent for Peter, his junior secretary, at the second hour after vespers' with no intermediary moment. Give us three sentences — Augustine sitting in the empty courtyard, Pelagius's cup in his hand (he drinks it, yes, but then what?) — before the time jump. The dread of the Peter sequence depends on feeling the weight of what Augustine has just decided. Let it land before the next scene begins.

The Mark scene needs his stutter to appear at one specific moment — perhaps when Augustine says 'The most important commission you will have had to date' — and then to disappear as he affirms he will discharge it. Show the stutter that hope suppresses. Make the reader feel how eagerly he boards that ship toward something he cannot know. The dread of his innocence is the chapter's final horror before 'He slept well,' and right now it's reported rather than felt.

Consider cutting the line 'The two facts were not in tension, in the sense that the bishop required of facts that they not be in tension.' It's the author being clever at the expense of letting the horror breathe. What precedes it — the inventory of what the sealed letters actually contain — is devastating on its own. The explanatory crack about Augustine's epistemology deflates the moment. End the paragraph at 'would not survive the second month of confinement.' Full stop. Then the next paragraph about the boy delivering in good faith will crush the reader without assistance.

sections.assessment: This chapter is doing something genuinely ambitious with dialogue: it uses a five-century-old theological debate as a thriller mechanism, with the conversation itself as the action sequence. That conceit mostly works, and when it works it's excellent — the subtext is devastating, the best exchanges are compressed and loaded, and the Peter scene is a near-masterclass in what King means by subtext. The chapter's central dialogue achievement is that we understand Augustine has decided to destroy Pelagius before Pelagius has left the courtyard, and neither man ever says this. That's real craft. The weak spots are structural and recurring: Pelagius's theological arguments unspool as full academic essays, which is a speech pattern no actual throat has ever produced; the briefings to Peter and Mark weaponize dialogue as an exposition-delivery system; and Mark, as a character, has no voice at all — he is a prop that says 'Father, I understand it' four times. The chapter earns its best grades on subtext and on what-is-NOT-said. It loses ground on length, on the naturalistic anchor that makes even formal historical registers feel spoken rather than written, and on the flatness of its secondary voices.
good_examples: 'The pears. I think we have all stolen pears, Bishop, when we were boys, but it is a great achievement to have written about them so that one cannot afterwards eat a pear without thinking of the will.' — Pelagius reveals himself completely in one sentence: humor, self-inclusion, genuine admiration, and the theological point delivered obliquely through a specific object. This is a man talking, not a doctrine walking.

'Will we meet again. / I think not. / I think not also.' — No question marks. Both men know. Neither says what they know. 'He said it in the tone of a man recording a probable fact' is the killer gloss. Four words of dialogue carry a death sentence. This is King's 'what is NOT said' operating at its highest level.

'Cirta first? / No. Rome first. Cirta when the Pelagian materials are clean.' — Two men communicating a conspiracy in eleven words while walking past each other. Possidius asks in half a question; Augustine answers in a policy decision. Neither slows down. The elision of every explanatory word is the whole point.

'Father, is that — ' He stopped. He did not finish the question. He waited. — Peter's half-question is the best beat in the chapter. The stylus pausing, the not-looking-up, the decision to ask or not ask — we watch a young man's moral life fork in real time. His eventual oblique question, 'is that the right way to set out a man's teaching,' is perfect: deniable, precise, and terrified.

'Pelagius. Sit.' — Augustine has been rehearsing 'a small Latin courtesy' and abandons it. What replaces it is two words with no courtesy in them at all. The bare imperative is a whole character, and it cost the writer nothing.
bad_examples: 'Then, Pelagius said, and now he leaned a small degree forward on the stool... what I do not understand in the position you have taken in your later writings is the further claim that the will, prior to grace, has no power...' continuing for six full paragraphs — Pelagius delivers a structured academic argument with three distinct sub-points, a running analogy, and a conclusion. No one speaks this way. Even a formal fifth-century rhetorician pauses, doubles back, waits for reaction. King's rule: a speech that could be submitted to a journal is not dialogue.

'The works are: a commentary on the Pauline epistles in thirteen books; a tract called De Natura which I have in three copies in this room, of which one is from Rome and two are from Carthage and which are, between them, complete; a long letter to a Roman matron called Demetrias...' — This is the As-You-Know-Bob problem in full vestments. Augustine is not actually talking to Peter; the author is talking to the reader through Augustine's mouth. Peter has presumably seen these materials on the shelf. A bishop briefing his own secretary would say 'You know the De Natura — the three copies on the shelf. The Demetrias letter in the sleeve.' The full inventory signals that the scene's real purpose is to inform the reader, not to direct the secretary.

'Father, I understand it. / Yes, Father. / I understand it, Father. / I understand the order, Father. / Father, I know it. I will discharge it. / Father.' — Mark speaks six times and says nothing. We are told he has 'a slight stutter on initial p's' that emerges when nervous, and then he is never nervous enough — or given language interesting enough — to make the stutter appear. The stutter is a promise the scene doesn't keep. Mark is an envelope.
suggestions: Cut Pelagius's theological speeches by half and give Augustine the space he's being denied — let him interrupt, qualify, push back in real time rather than waiting for Pelagius to finish delivering a treatise. The theological argument is more dangerous, and more dramatic, if it's interrupted, not declaimed.

Make the stutter appear. When Augustine tells Mark this is 'the most important commission you will have had to date,' Mark should stumble on whatever initial consonant his nerves produce. One moment of real physical speech would make Mark a person instead of a plot function and would give the scene's irony — Mark delivering letters he doesn't understand — actual bite.

In the Peter briefing, replace the inventory of Pelagian works with a single telling exchange: let Peter ask one question that shows he's already read something he shouldn't have, or doesn't know something he should. A gap in knowledge, rather than an informed list, would establish the scene's power dynamic and move information to the reader through conflict rather than recitation.

The Possidius exchange — 'Cirta first? No. Rome first.' — is the chapter's best dialogue because it trusts the reader completely. Apply that same compression to the Mark briefing: Augustine says less, the reader infers more, and the gap between what's said and what's happening becomes the reader's active experience rather than a fact to be disclosed six paragraphs later in the narrator's summary of what the letters actually contained.

Consider giving Pelagius one piece of speech that is slightly wrong in register — a word that's too informal, a moment where the formal Latin courtesy slips and something raw comes through. Right now both men are perfectly composed throughout. One hairline crack in Pelagius's surface would make Augustine's decision to destroy him both harder and more interesting.

sections.assessment: This chapter is architecturally ambitious and mostly earns its ambition. The writer understands something essential: pace is not speed, it's control. The chapter moves through five distinct gear-shifts — the surprise arrival, the theological combat, the cold interior decision, the corruption of Peter, and the funeral elegy — and the transitions between them are clean and purposeful. The white space use (ruled breaks, one-sentence paragraphs, italicized interior thought) shows a writer who understands that the page itself is an instrument. The best moments hit hard precisely because the prose has been slow and precise in the paragraphs before them. However, the central theological debate — about 1,200 words of philosophical argument — is the chapter's structural weak point. It is intellectually brilliant and thematically necessary, but the pacing here loses its grip. King's rule: even when characters talk, the reader must feel the floor vibrating. The debate needs more physical interruption — more of the courtyard, more of Pelagius's hands and the untouched figs — to sustain the thriller register. The Peter and Mark sequences, both excellent individually, mirror each other too closely in structure and pacing: same procedural setup, same withheld revelation, same cool final paragraph. The second landing hits softer than the first. The chapter's final three words — 'He slept well' — are perfect King: short sentence, massive weight, scene closed without ceremony. The chapter would benefit from being about 15% shorter in its middle third.
good_examples: 'Augustine set down the pen.' — One sentence. One paragraph. Everything stops. The writer understands white space as emphasis: after a dense, circling paragraph about the gatekeeper, this lands like a door slamming.

'If I let him go to Palestine and let his writings circulate with the obvious truth of his face — within ten years there will be a Pelagian Latin Church that will eat my work. The work cannot be eaten. The work is the wrapper. The wrapper has to hold. I will have to destroy him.' — The chapter's gear-shift. Everything before this builds toward this. The internal rhythm here — three short declarative sentences after the long conditional — mirrors the cold mechanical click of a decision being made.

'He slept well.' — Three words closing the chapter. King would call this perfect. The reader has watched Augustine corrupt a young man, dispatch an assassination through sealed letters, weep genuinely at a funeral, and return to cut the qualifications out of a good man's arguments. Three words do the moral accounting.

'The water tasted of nothing in particular.' — After the charged, loaded gesture of drinking from Pelagius's cup, this deflation is exactly right. The prose refuses sentimentality at the moment sentimentality is most available.

The italicized Eraclius exchange — 'Bishop. Pelagius is at the cloister.' / 'Where is he now.' / 'In the cloister.' — The compression here accelerates the scene without rushing it. No attribution tags. Each line a beat. This is the chapter finding its thriller register before settling into the longer scene.
bad_examples: From 'The conversation began as the conversation of any two readers...' through 'He drank, finally, a small mouthful of the water from the wooden cup' — roughly 300 words of preamble before the actual argument begins. The detail about Pelagius reading the Confessions twice in Rome and letting two correspondents copy it is good character work, but it arrives before the tension is established. King would cut or compress this: earn the small talk by putting us inside it faster.

'He did not, in three hours, sit down or accept food.' — This is stated once here, then the same observation arrives again: 'He did not eat the figs. He did not break bread.' The repetition isn't structural — it doesn't add new information or deepen the beat. It dilutes. Pick the better moment and trust it.

The Mark sequence (the Rome commission) is structurally identical to the Peter sequence: setup, procedural instruction, withheld revelation, cold closing paragraph. Both are well-written. The second landing is weaker because the reader has already received this rhythm once. The Mark section needs a different gear — sharper compression, or a structural surprise — to earn its place after Peter.

'He spoke of the *De Doctrina Christiana* and the *De Trinitate* and the early books of the *Enarrationes in Psalmos*.' — The citation-dropping here momentarily pulls the reader out of the scene into a bibliography. The pacing flattens. The works named are inert to most readers; the sentence earns nothing the surrounding paragraphs haven't already established.
suggestions: Break up the theological debate with more frequent returns to the physical courtyard. Every three or four exchanges, cut to a sentence about the fig tree, the companion against the wall, the untouched bread, the movement of light. These interruptions don't need to be long — two or three words of white space and a sentence — but they hold the thriller register and remind the reader that Augustine is measuring, not just arguing.

Compress or restructure the Mark sequence to differentiate it from the Peter sequence. Consider cutting the provisioning instructions and the stutter detail, and going directly from the commission to the cold closing paragraph about the Egyptian desert. Let the second revelation land faster and harder than the first — escalation, not repetition.

The line 'He had not yet made a single statement that was not true' is doing important thematic work, but it's buried in the middle of a paragraph of physical observation. Break it out as its own sentence and its own paragraph. Let it breathe. That line is one of the chapter's moral fulcrums.

Trim the opening approach paragraph. The gatekeeper's unfamiliarity with names outside the African church, and Eraclius crossing himself, are both strong details — keep them. The rest of the routing detail (lay parlour vs. cloister) can be cut or compressed. The paragraph currently delays 'Augustine set down the pen' by about forty words it doesn't need.

The surveillance exchange at the post-house ('A man came up the western road yesterday...') is excellent compressed plotting but arrives immediately after the news of Lawrence's death, with no transition. One sentence of interiority — Augustine absorbing the news, or the silence on the road — would make the shift feel like Augustine's mind rather than the chapter's convenience.

sections.assessment: This chapter does something genuinely difficult: it makes its theological surface argument — free will versus grace — into the exact mechanism by which its real theme operates. Augustine, the man who argues the will is enslaved and cannot lift itself toward good without prevenient grace, freely chooses to destroy a man who has just demonstrated, using Augustine's own sentence, that this argument is self-undermining. The irony is structural, not decorative. It was built into the bones before the first word. That's the rarest kind of thematic work — when the story IS the argument about the story's own subject. The subtext is devastating throughout: institutional power routinely destroys what it cannot absorb; truth loses to systems that need it to lose; the young are recruited into corruption one small decision at a time; and — the darkest note — a man can be genuinely sincere and simultaneously monstrous, and can know it, and can sleep. The moment Augustine thinks 'This is what I do to the young men' and then does it anyway is extraordinary: the self-awareness is complete and completely impotent. Where the chapter falls short of its full potential is in its thriller infrastructure. The disc inside Cassian's Conferences, the face seen from the bell tower, the surveillance network — these are plot engines, but they haven't yet been recruited into the thematic conversation. They sit alongside the real meaning rather than deepening it. The Mark scene, while competently executed, is more mechanical than the Augustine-Peter scene because Peter's moral corruption is the theme made flesh, while Mark's good faith delivery is just plot. The Cirta funeral section contributes the important note that Augustine's sincerity is genuine — not a mask — but the theme has already been established more powerfully in the writing room. King's rule: once you've landed your theme, trust it. Don't re-land it in a different setting to make sure the reader got it.
good_examples: 'He thought one thing only, and he thought it in the steady internal voice of the work: If I let him go to Palestine and let his writings circulate with the obvious truth of his face — within ten years there will be a Pelagian Latin Church that will eat my work. The work cannot be eaten. The work is the wrapper. The wrapper has to hold. I will have to destroy him.' — The thematic hinge of the entire chapter. The man who argues the will cannot move itself toward good makes an act of pure will. The man who argues against Pelagius's face being a problem concedes, privately, that Pelagius's face is the problem. Truth loses to preservation, and the moment is reported in the flat bureaucratic cadence of administration. That tonal choice is the horror.

'By the third month he will be a young man of a particular kind, and the kind will not unlearn what it has learned tonight in the next forty years of his life. This is what I do to the young men.' — Theme delivered through character knowledge, not authorial commentary. Augustine sees exactly what he's doing, names it, and does it. That gap between self-knowledge and self-correction is the chapter's real subject — and it emerges entirely from who this man is in this situation.

'The two facts were not in tension, in the sense that the bishop required of facts that they not be in tension.' — One sentence doing the work of a paragraph on motivated reasoning. It's funny, devastating, and structurally self-referential: a sentence about the refusal to see contradiction that is itself constructed to prevent contradiction. King would call this the kind of line that earns its author a free afternoon.

'Your cor inquietum — the restless heart that did not rest until it rested in God — is, I think, the heart that I have been describing... The verb is not passive in the sense you require it to be passive.' — Pelagius hands Augustine his own load-bearing sentence and shows it means the opposite of what Augustine needs it to mean. The subtext is that Augustine has been lying to himself about his own text for sixteen years. The subsequent internal admission — 'he had subsequently been progressively pretending the line did not bear' — is the most honest thing Augustine thinks in the chapter, and it leads directly to the decision to destroy the man who made him see it.
bad_examples: 'route a man whose conviction had not yet been secured to a region of the Egyptian western desert in which a man of fifty-two who had walked barefoot from the Hippo harbour would not survive the second month of confinement.' — The callback to the bare feet is excellent subtext. But the surrounding clause — 'would not survive the second month' — is the author underlining the horror the reader already fully understands. The discovery that two of seven letters are a death warrant has already landed. Explaining its lethality in clinical terms is the writer not trusting the reader, which is the one unforgivable craft failure. Cut the survival prediction. Let the Egyptian desert do its own work.

'The meaning had not at any point been incompatible with the work that was waiting on the upper shelf.' — This line, closing the Cirta section, re-states the sincerity-plus-corruption theme that was established with far more power in the writing room scene. The Cirta funeral earns its place as emotional texture, but this closing sentence is the author reaching back to make sure the thematic connection was noticed. It wasn't needed. The reader is there. The 'He slept well' ending is the right instinct — trust the declarative, don't explain it.
suggestions: The disc inside Cassian's Conferences appears twice — checked before the meeting, checked on return from Cirta — but it has no thematic weight yet because we don't know what it is or why it matters. In King's best work, objects carry meaning proportional to their mystery. Right now the disc is pure plot. Find one detail — its shape, its origin, what it represents to Augustine — that makes it speak to the free will/grace question or to the question of what is hidden and what is shown. The checking of it should feel like Augustine checking his own soul and finding it still where he left it.

The Mark scene needs one moment of moral texture to match the Peter scene — not full development, but one beat that acknowledges what Mark is carrying without knowing it. Currently Mark is a prop. Even a single line of his interiority — his pride at being trusted, his genuine belief in the bishop's goodness — would make his good faith devastating rather than merely functional. The contrast between Peter's slow moral education and Mark's complete innocence is a theme waiting to be written.

The surveillance subplot (the man on the western road, Stephen at the bell tower) is currently pure thriller infrastructure. Consider giving it one line of thematic resonance. Augustine runs a surveillance network on heretics and suspects — but so did Pelagius's people, apparently. If both men are watching each other, the chapter gains another ironic layer: two men who just spent three hours in genuine intellectual communion, each immediately deploying the mechanisms of institutional paranoia. That's not a plot note, it's a theme note.

Augustine's Cicero speech to Peter on the duty of the forensic orator is the weakest passage in the chapter because it is Augustine explaining his own self-deception at length, which dilutes the horror of Peter's 'I will do it.' Cut the speech by a third. The reader understands the rationalization by the second sentence. Let Peter's quiet acquiescence carry more weight by making Augustine's justification less fully elaborated. The sophistication of the lie is more frightening when we have to finish it ourselves.

sections.assessment: This chapter does what almost no historical fiction manages: it doesn't costume a modern story in period dress. It thinks from *inside* 411 CE. The 'brand names' aren't Pepsi and Chevrolet — they're the Demetrias letter's circulation pattern in African households of substance, the ship Salvia on Friday's tide, the deacon Justin cultivated for two years in the praetorian prefecture. The theological debate is the *actual* debate, the texts are the *actual* texts, the positions are rendered with the kind of fidelity that makes the moral horror of the excerpt-selection project feel inevitable rather than contrived. Pelagius's calloused soles, the British manner of cropping hair at the jaw, the absence of even a small bronze pectoral cross — these aren't atmosphere, they're character: a man produced by a specific monastic tradition in a specific province at a specific moment. The late-empire Roman bureaucratic network (grain office, master of the offices, consistory) grounds the political thriller machinery in the actual machinery of the period. The one genuine anachronism — 'bootstraps' — lands like a dropped wrench in a cathedral, precisely because everything else is so clean. King's rule holds: one wrong detail makes the reader aware they're reading prose. The chapter earns an A- rather than an A because that single slip is the kind of error that a writer at this level should not make, and because two or three other formulations flirt with a 21st-century editorial register that the period does not support.
good_examples: 'a thin white sky of the kind that comes at Hippo in October when the autumn wind has just begun to pull off the sea, and the courtyard held the light without holding the heat' — this is not Mediterranean-generic. This is a specific sky in a specific season in a specific coastal city. The distinction between light and heat in October Hippo is the kind of detail that only works when the writer knows where they are.

'He had read the *Confessions* twice in Rome and once aloud to a small house community in Aquileia. He had brought it back to Britain on a journey home in his fortieth year and had let two of his correspondents copy it, by which means it was now circulating, in two close copies and one looser one, in a little ring of houses on the western coast that read it on Saturday evenings in lieu of the office.' — This is the culture of manuscript circulation in 411 CE rendered with absolute period fidelity. The distinction between two close copies and one looser one is a scribe's distinction. The ring of houses reading it on Saturday evenings in lieu of the office is a sociological fact about early British Christianity. This paragraph could not have been written in any other era.

'His hair was grey, cropped at the line of the jaw in the British manner. His face was the colour of a man who lived out of doors... His feet were bare. The soles on the courtyard stones were as calloused as the feet of a working ploughman.' — Pelagius is a product of his era, his province, his monastic tradition. These are not costume details. They are the marks of a specific theological and ascetic practice that the British churches carried and the African churches did not.

'*Inquietum est cor nostrum, donec requiescat in te*. That is your line. The verb is *requiescat*. Let it rest. The verb is not passive in the sense you require it to be passive.' — Deploying the actual Latin of the actual first paragraph of the Confessions as the hinge of the theological argument is exactly right. This is the intellectual 'brand name' of 411 CE: not a car model but a sentence that has been circulating in manuscripts for sixteen years and that the man who wrote it has been progressively misreading.

'the deacon Justin in the office of the praetorian prefecture had been cultivated for two years in expectation of being asked to facilitate such placements; that the request now being made through Justin would, in the year of its eventual execution, route a man whose conviction had not yet been secured to a region of the Egyptian western desert' — the late Roman imperial bureaucracy as a weapon. The grain office, the master of the offices, the consistory, Ostia, the Lateran: this is the actual political-administrative texture of the western empire in the decade after Alaric's sack. The thriller machinery is period-authentic machinery.
bad_examples: 'the danger of teaching men they could lift themselves by their own bootstraps' — this idiom is nineteenth-century American in origin, traceable to no earlier than the 1800s. Augustine of Hippo did not have bootstraps as a conceptual or literal referent. In a chapter that is otherwise impeccably grounded, this is the one place where a modern writer's automatic idiom-reach trips the wire. The sentence could be 'the danger of teaching men that they could by their own effort lift what grace alone can lift' or simply cut — the theological point has already been made more precisely in the preceding passage.

'he had subsequently been progressively pretending the line did not bear' — the formulation 'progressively pretending' has a slightly contemporary psychoanalytic flavor that doesn't quite fit the internal voice of a fifth-century bishop. Augustine's self-knowledge in this chapter is otherwise rendered in the language of the period — *cor inquietum*, the *massa damnata*, the motion of the will. One sentence of modern introspective idiom breaks the register. Something closer to 'and that he had, in the ten years since, been arguing the line away from' would hold the period texture.
suggestions: Run a systematic pass looking for idioms with datable origins — 'bootstraps' is the flagged example but there may be others embedded in the internal voice passages. The rule is: if the phrasing could appear in a 2025 essay without raising an eyebrow, it probably needs scrutiny in a 411 CE interior monologue.

The chapter currently gives the feast of the African martyrs as the date-anchor but does not specify which feast. In October 411 CE there are specific options — the feast of Perpetua and Felicitas falls in March, but the commemoration calendar for the African church in October has specific entries. If the writer knows which feast and deploys it by name, the temporal grounding tightens one notch further, and the irony of Augustine receiving Pelagius on a martyr's feast gains an additional layer.

Peter's wax tablet note to Augustine — 'I have erred on the side of fidelity to the qualifications' — is superb cultural texture: the scriptorium culture, the vocabulary of the copyist, the deference of the junior scholar. Consider whether the phrasing 'will not satisfy you in their first form' could be slightly more archaic in register without becoming precious, since this is the one extended piece of period dialogue rendered in indirect free style that slightly flattens.

The material culture inventory of Pelagius — the cup, the wool, the bare feet, the absence of a pectoral cross — is excellently handled. Consider doing the same systematic material inventory for the writing room itself: the specific composition of the ink (lampblack and gum), the kind of lamp (olive oil, wick material), the texture of the vellum or papyrus in the rolls. King's principle is that the reader should be able to smell the room. Right now we can see it clearly; one more sensory layer from the right material detail would make us able to smell it.

sections.assessment: This is a chapter operating at a very high level of prose discipline. The style is deliberate, paratactic, and flat in the way that does enormous emotional work by restraint — the ecclesiastical precision of the syntax mirrors Augustine's administrative, controlled mind. Adverbs are almost entirely absent. Dialogue tags are either dropped or 'said.' Clichés are nowhere. The prose is largely invisible in King's sense: you notice the betrayal of Pelagius, not the sentences describing it. The chapter's final line — 'He slept well.' — is a masterclass in the value of three words. The inner monologue where Augustine decides to destroy Pelagius ('The work cannot be eaten. The work is the wrapper. The wrapper has to hold. I will have to destroy him.') is cold and precise and does not flinch. These are the marks of a writer in control. The flaws are real but few: a handful of sentences call attention to themselves as writing; a handful of moments explain what the scene has already shown; one notable syntactic clunk in the physical description. The 2nd-draft-minus-10% principle would improve this, but not dramatically.
good_examples: 'He slept well.' — Three words to end the chapter. No gloss, no judgment, no adverb. King's entire philosophy in one sentence. The reader supplies the horror. The writer gets out of the way.

'The water tasted of nothing in particular. He set the cup down, stood, and went to the writing room.' — Augustine drinks the water Pelagius left, and it tastes of nothing. The prose matches: no symbolism pushed, no meaning italicized. The action is the meaning.

'If I let him go to Palestine and let his writings circulate with the obvious truth of his face — within ten years there will be a Pelagian Latin Church that will eat my work. The work cannot be eaten. The work is the wrapper. The wrapper has to hold. I will have to destroy him.' — The word 'eat' is doing massive work. The cold managerial logic of 'The wrapper has to hold' followed immediately by 'I will have to destroy him' is devastating precisely because it is not dramatized. Invisible prose doing visible damage.

'Augustine sat with the line. The line was his own line. The line had been his own line for sixteen years... Pelagius was now handing it back to him with a reading that the line plainly bore, that he himself had written the line to bear, and that he had subsequently been progressively pretending the line did not bear.' — The anaphora on 'the line' mimics how an unwelcome argument circles in a trained mind. This is form serving content without announcing itself.

'The boy's stylus paused on the wax. He did not lift his head.' — Physical detail doing the work of an emotion the writer has the discipline not to name. King's first law: show, don't tell, and the showing here is precise and economical.

'from somewhere inside the cloister a voice was reading aloud — Stephen, probably, at the prayers — and the cadence of the reading came faintly across the courtyard and went away.' — 'And went away.' That's it. No comment on the irony of prayer floating through the air while Augustine plots a man's death. The prose trusts the reader entirely.
bad_examples: 'had then walked to the writing room with an expression that was not customary for him' — Eight words where two would do. 'An expression he never wore' is better. 'With a look that stopped Augustine's pen' is better still. 'That was not customary for him' is a circumlocution that calls attention to the fact that the writer couldn't find the sharper word.

'Inside the part of himself that was not a bishop he flinched.' — This sentence calls attention to itself as writing. It is trying to be elegant and it is instead visible. The two-part self (bishop/man) has already been established throughout the chapter. Cut everything before 'he flinched.' Just: 'He flinched.' The reader has earned the plain verb.

'The older man was tall and lean and was tall in the manner of a man who had grown into his height through the discipline of fasting' — The double 'tall' ('was tall and lean and was tall') is the one syntactic stumble in an otherwise precise chapter. It reads like a first-draft sentence that forgot it had already said 'tall.' King's rule: every word earns its place, and 'tall' has not earned two.

'He had not yet learned to read his bishop's pauses for the matter behind the procedure.' — The narrator steps in to explain what the scene has already shown perfectly. Peter's stylus paused. Peter did not lift his head. Peter waited. We know Peter doesn't understand the pause. This sentence is the author's anxiety that the reader might have missed it.
suggestions: Cut 'Inside the part of himself that was not a bishop he' and render it simply: 'He flinched.' The bishop/man division is already doing its work throughout the chapter; the sentence exists to protect a reading the scene has already secured.

Fix the double 'tall': 'The older man was tall and lean, the height earned through the discipline of fasting and standing through long offices' — or any construction that doesn't repeat the adjective within three words of itself.
Cut 'He had not yet learned to read his bishop's pauses for the matter behind the procedure.' The scene earns this without the gloss. Trust Peter's paused stylus.

Consider cutting 'The two facts were not in tension, in the sense that the bishop required of facts that they not be in tension.' This is the best line in the chapter in one reading — but it also explains what the entire Mark scene has just dramatized. King would say: you already wrote this scene. You don't need to write it again in one sentence. If you keep it, it should be the last sentence of the paragraph, not the second-to-last — land on it and stop.

In the physical description of Pelagius, 'in the manner of a man greeting an equal who is also his elder' can lose 'who is also his elder' — the elder relationship has been established; the qualifier is defensive filler.

The stutter introduced for Mark ('a slight stutter on initial p's') pays off only in 'The stutter had not appeared.' This is a detail doing one small cosmetic job. Either give it more weight — let the stutter appear once under pressure in the scene, showing what its absence means at the close — or cut it entirely and trust Mark's smile to carry the note.

overall_assessment: King-style analysis complete. Overall grade: B+. Strengths: Deep POV & Interior Monologue, Backstory & Characterization, Tension & Dread Building, Pacing & Structure, Themes & Subtext, Cultural Specificity & Time Period, Prose Polish (King Polish).
